It is my privilege on behalf of the Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology Government of India to welcome you all to the pre-event for the upcoming Mega India AI impact summit 2026 and for announcing several key initiatives under the India AI mission aimed at building the AI innovation ecosystem of India. The government of India is organizing the India AI impact summit in February 2026 at Bharat Mandapur, New Delhi that will be inaugurated by our honorable prime minister Shri Narendra Modi G. The summit will focus on the impact of AI on three sutras, people, planet and progress and would bring together heads of states, global leaders, policy makers, researchers, industry experts and innovators for working out a plan for leveraging AI for the larger humanitarian good and creating value. We are indeed honored to have with us honorable union minister Shri Ashwini Vashnav gi principal scientific adviser to government of India professor Ajay Kumar Sud Shri S Krishnan secretary matei professor Steuart J Russell from the University of California Berkeley Mr. Yan Talin, founder Skype, Miss J Dejani Kosh Ding, distinguished fellow Niti Aayog, Miss Kavita Bhya, COO, India AI and group coordinator AI and emerging technology division, Matei and Abishek Singh, additional secretary Matei, CEO India AI and DZ NIC and we are grateful that for our esteemed dignitaries to have joined us along with policy makers, industry leaders and innovators who are shaping the future. future of AI in India. To commence with the program, I would like to invite Shriy Bishek Singh to kindly welcome this August audience. Thank you. Thank you. Honorable Minister for Electronics and IT, Railways, Information Broadcasting, Shashini Vashnoji, our Secretary Krishna Serji, , Professor Sud, Principal Scientific Adviser, distinguished fellow from Niti Aayog and our strong supporter DJ Jani, Professor Russell, Yon Talin, my colleague Kavita, distinguished guests, senior officers from government of India, state governments, industry leaders, startup champions and key partners in the AI story of India. It's my honor and privilege to welcome you all today at this event where we are formally launching the AI impact summit activities. The logo for the for the summit and the momentum towards trying to build up a first AI summit that is happening in the global south. We are we have moved on from where we started at BCHley Park in November 23 onwards to Seoul and to Paris action summit where we co-chared the summit. Our honorable prime minister Shinarind Modi G and President Macarron addressed the session there and laid down the vision for a future in which AI will help solve the challenges of humanity. help build build solutions that can help extend reach of services to all and try to create a global governance framework in which this technology works for the larger good for all and limit the harms it can

So across this whole seven pillared India's AI mission strategy also we have made certain progress and some of the announcements will be made about what we have been able to do in the last few months. The last announcement we did in March wherein four startups were supported for building foundation models. Today a few more will be announced by our honorable minister. We also look forward to announcing all the activities that will take up as part of the India impact summit. The details of which will be unveiled today by our honorable minister. But in the days to come, we'll look forward to involvement of the entire ecosystem in building the story of India around the impact summit. The whole world is looking up to India. We are trying to give a summit that will not only be a forum where people come and talk but a forum wherein action plan for the future will be laid down and together we'll be able to move on this journey forward. With these words, I once again welcome our honorable minister who has been guiding us in this journey and all of you who have joined us here today on this occasion to motivate all the minds that are working towards realizing India's AI dream. Thank you. Thank you so much sir. This is the pace at which we are working at which MIT is working and you know everyone talks about India AI mission and how transformative it is because it is laying the digital foundation or the laying the infrastructure for India's AI ambitions and AI aspirations. I just have one thought to that and I was thinking about it is what does this digital infrastructure do? What does digital infrastructure for AI really mean? I think what India AI mission is doing by laying the digital infrastructure for AI for India, it is democratizing not just AI but it is democratizing access to intelligence to expertise to every single Indian citizen. And imagine the power of that. Imagine the power of all of us, not just the ones in the room, but the ones sitting in villages, ones sitting in districts being empowered to get access to intelligence, whatever expertise they want real time at their fingertips. Imagine what this means for citizen empowerment, for productivity, for overall growth of the country. And I think that's why I strongly believe that the India AI mission is not just laying the foundation for the future of AI for India but it is laying the foundation for Vix Barrett. That is what is needed for India to be a developed nation. So again all the best Abishek and Minister if you permit I'm going to embarrass my dear friend Abishek here today for a second by wishing him a very happy birthday. My colleague Abhishek Singh very many happy returns of the day Abhishek and my colleague in matei Kavita Bhya ladies and gentlemen u you know it's it's quite amazing how anything an event about AI how quickly it fills the hall just about anytime you want to have one it just shows the degree of hunger the degree of interest in this particular area and what people want to find out more and more about. The curiosity, the interest in something which most of us believe could be one of the most significant moments in history in terms of how it can transform the entire economy, how it can transform various other social aspects is is truly to be believed. And this is the enthusiasm and the way that this attracts so much of not just attention but so much of interest amongst people who want to know more and this is what makes India such a critical place in terms of the global talent which is there for AI. they say that the AI Stanford index each year puts it out and we we supposed to have one of the highest penetrations of AI skills in the Indian workforce and in the last year there's been almost a 33.4% increase in AI hiring in India. So all of this basically means that this is an area which and this is a space on which we need to build and move forward and sometimes not being the first off the blocks is not such a bad thing. you catch up on a number of other elements which can really work for you in terms of figuring out where things are going wrong. a bad thing. you catch up on a number of other elements which can really work for you in terms of figuring out where things are going wrong. How can you leapfrog on some of the developments which have already taken place? How can you actually base what you do further by standing on the shoulders of giants? So in every sense of the word, I think being a little late off the blocks is not a bad thing at all in the AI's case and in AI's case and I think that is being proved as we follow literature in the recent past that is getting proved more and more and there is still of course a long way to go and there are multiple ways in which India can develop from this. The model that we've developed under the India mission is a unique model is different. There is government involvement but to a very large extent there's also the private sector which is involved in this entire enterprise whether it is to create additional compute whether it is to make India's new models whether it is to look at use cases and applications which can be developed and deployed in each of these areas we are engaging very closely with the private sector through a number of innovative means and models which again is unique in any one program and that is what we have attempted and we've been successful in doing likewise the impact summit India is chairing the impact summit this year the AI summit fourth in the series the first three and and in the way in which this has transitioned from being a safety summit in UK in Bletchley Park in November of 2023 to becoming an action summit in Paris in February 24 to 25 and Now in 26th February becoming the impact summit. It just goes to show how things have progressed, how things are moving on and how India is now focused on what could be the positive outcomes of AI. What are ways in which we can actually ensure that AI works for people. It is it it needs to be a decentralized movement. It needs to be an inclusive movement. It needs to work for the people of the country. We need to deliver something positive for everybody in society. We need to deliver something positive for every country of the world. And that is the basis and that is the objective on which the AI impact summit is being put together. We we are working in ways that can actually enable this to happen. the process of working on each of the independent strands of the AI impact summit have been have have commenced in terms of what are the areas we need to work on. The the emphasis is

together. We we are working in ways that can actually enable this to happen. the process of working on each of the independent strands of the AI impact summit have been have have commenced in terms of what are the areas we need to work on. The the emphasis is are the three sutras of the AI summit in terms of what is it that we are attempting there. And the areas that we work on, the themes or the chakras that we are working on are human capital which is very critical especially in a country like India as I already mentioned. It is about inclusion and making sure that everyone gets included in this process. It is about safe and trusted AI so that you can trust it. the you can feel protected, you can feel comfortable using AI in various walks of life. It's about resilience because resilience the one lesson that we've learned through COVID and through all the geopolitical turmoil that we see in the world is resilience is critical. How do you build models which are resilient? How do you build models where you know energy and energy resilience, environmental resilience, sustainability, each of those aspects needs to be addressed? It's about developing science ultimately. How do we apply these new tools to deepen our understanding in scientific areas and harnessing more scientific discoveries to for the betterment of humanity. So that's that's a clear area that we need to work on. It's about democratizing the availability of AI resources for everybody across the world. How do you make sure that adequate compute is available? How do you make sure that people are able to look at models? How do we make sure that applications which are developed can actually get deployed across the world even in the poorer parts of the world and this is something that India as one of the leaders in the global south can truly ensure. So these are the seven themes around which the AI summit is being built and that is a work which is going on as we as we speak and we near the summit in February. You will see all of these details fleshed out in greater a greater detail and in in greater depth and that would be the stage at which India would truly make its mark in this space of AI and AI's governance. I think that is what is important. Harnessing the good of AI for the betterment of humanity would be the focus that India would have and how we do it and what would be the tools that we use to do it is the effort that we are currently engaged in and involved in and each of these events contributes to that effort as India AI mission progresses and today you will see the way in which it is moving forward. What is it that we are able to contribute

do it is the effort that we are currently engaged in and involved in and each of these events contributes to that effort as India AI mission progresses and today you will see the way in which it is moving forward. What is it that we are able to contribute able to contribute to the welfare of the people of this country? How is it that we are able to reach out? That is the message which needs to be carried and that that is a message that events of this nature truly serve. So once again, I'd like to just thank the honorable minister for being here and all the visiting dignitaries who have joined us at very short notice to be present here and share their knowledge and share their perspective with us. Thank you for joining us. Thank you all. [Applause] Thanks very much sir for setting the stage for a transformative event. I would now like to request renowned scientist Professor Stuart J. Russell from the University of California Berkeley to kindly give an address on AI some thoughts. [Applause] Thank you very much. thank you for the invitation to be here. and thank you to the ministry for taking on the organization of the summit. and I think based on the wisdom of the remarks that we've heard so far, the India AI mission is off to a very good start. and as was mentioned I think we are at a critical point in human history and this summit can ensure that humanity has a say in how human history unfolds and that will be its most important function. So, we're here to talk about AI, but it's important to remember that AI is not a single thing. Imagine instead that we were talking about physics and someone would say, "Okay, we need a governance plan for physics." It sort of wouldn't really make sense because physics is many different things. Everything from electricity to atomic weapons and cell phones and lots of other things come from physics. The same is true for AI. And so when you hear a leading technology executive say if you're against world destroying AI, you're against better medical diagnosis. This is not true. You can be against world destroying AI and in favor of better medical diagnosis because these are two different kinds of technologies. And if you look at maybe the biggest contribution that AI has made so far to our world which I think is Alphafold. So Alphafold is a system for predicting the structures of proteins that has revolutionized structural biology, biochemistry and the design of new pharmaceuticals. That technology has nothing to do with the development of world destroying artificial general intelligence. and in my own work, we were able to develop a new monitoring system for the nuclear testban treaty, which is much less well known than alphafall, but I still think it's useful. and again it has no connection with the kind of

development of world destroying artificial general intelligence. and in my own work, we were able to develop a new monitoring system for the nuclear testban treaty, which is much less well known than alphafall, but I still think it's useful. and again it has no connection with the kind of is so much talked about in the press. And when we talk about health care, when we talk about education, weather forecasting, improved scientific research, advice for small businesses and farmers, none of these advances require artificial general intelligence. So we need to separate how we think about policy how we think about benefits how we think about regulation. we must understand that this is not a unitary single technology that we're talking about. So I work nowadays I worked for 50 years actually in AI of one kind or another. For most of that career, I was just working on trying to make AI systems more effective so that they could be more useful to human beings. In the last decade or so, I've worked on AI safety. AI safety means how do we make AI systems that do not harm human beings? And that includes not discriminating against them, not making mistakes in their medical diagnosis and not deciding that they should be killed in the form of lethal autonomous weapons. And the complement of not harming is obviously providing benefit. So the the technology that I work on is called provably beneficial AI. AI systems where we can actually prove mathematically that the system will be of benefit to human beings. And so when I hear that you can have safety or you can have benefits that in some sense there is an opposition between these two concepts it seems to me that this is a fallacy. In fact you can have benefits only if you have safety. The nuclear industry found this out in 1986 when the nuclear plant at Chernobyl exploded. And if you look at the number of nuclear plants that have been built on Earth each year, it increased steadily up to about 50 new plants per year in 1986. And then it dropped to effectively zero. And from then up until the early 2000s, there were essentially no new nuclear plants built on Earth. And so we didn't get the benefits of nuclear power because we didn't attend to the safety of nuclear power enough. Now there are many forms of governance that can promote safety. It doesn't just have to be regulation. Let me pick one example. Liability. The ability of someone who's been harmed to obtain redress in the courts from the person they believe has harmed them is a form of regulation or a form of governance if you like that actually facilitates the function of markets. It is an innovationpromoting kind of governance. But at the moment, if your child is convinced to commit suicide by an AI system, the company that developed that system

them is a form of regulation or a form of governance if you like that actually facilitates the function of markets. It is an innovationpromoting kind of governance. But at the moment, if your child is convinced to commit suicide by an AI system, the company that developed that system disclaim all liability for what happened to your child. I think this immunity from liability is causing a dysfunction in the markets. If you think about it, why would you not want to be liable? Well, you don't want to be liable if you think your product is doing more harm than good. But if you think your product is doing more harm than good, then why are you selling it? So, I think liability and preventing the disclaiming of liability would be a huge step forward to improving our markets for this kind of technology. But I can't really avoid the elephant in the room which is AGI or artificial general intelligence. So this means AI systems that exceed human ability in essentially all tasks. And this is the technology in which the major AI companies are planning to invest $3 trillion over the next three years. This is 50 to 100 times larger than the Manhattan project which produced nuclear weapons during World War II. It is by far the largest technology project that the human race has ever undertaken. And at the moment we have a number of unanswered questions about this project. And I think the lack of answers makes it very difficult difficult for governments to know how to form policy in this area. The first unanswered question is will it work? My own view actually is that there's probably a 75% chance that it will fail that it won't deliver the kind of capabilities that they're hoping for. and then the investment values the market values of those companies will collapse. Another unanswered question is how does it work? The companies that are developing it really have very little idea what is going on inside their systems. They are not designed. They are grown and we don't understand how they work inside. And then if it works, can we control it? At the moment, the answer is no. We have no idea how to control systems of that level of capability. In fact, we have no idea how to control the systems we're already building. And then if it works and if we can control it, do we actually want it? Do we want to live in a world? Do you want your children to live in a world where the vast majority of economic output, the vast majority of scientific and literary and creative output is produced by artificial intelligence systems. I think one of the dangers besides the lack of human purpose that might result is what we might call diseducation. That people would lose the incentive because there's no longer any economic or social purpose to it. They would lose

and creative output is produced by artificial intelligence systems. I think one of the dangers besides the lack of human purpose that might result is what we might call diseducation. That people would lose the incentive because there's no longer any economic or social purpose to it. They would lose you've seen the film Wally, you understand that this results in the infeeblement of our species. Now, if you go back over human history, we have expended approximately 1 trillion person years in passing what we know to the next generation. in teaching our children to understand what we understand and then to add to our understanding and pass it on to their children. And in fact, several of our technologies were passed on to us by prehuman species who also passed it on over millions of years. Once we break that chain, it may not be possible to put it back together again. So I mentioned earlier that education is an area where AI can deliver enormous value without constituting artificial general intelligence. And I think this is true. So this area of education is perhaps the biggest benefit that AI can deliver to the human race in this decade. every child on earth could have access to a highly skilled individualized tutor that will help them realize their full potential. So I think we can look to education as a litmus test for how we the human race are going to handle this challenge. Are we going to simply give up and say well let the AI system do it. The AI system can do my homework. the AI system can teach my class or are we going to use AI as a tool to make ourselves better to increase our own capabilities and ambitions? Thank you. Honorary Minister dear experts distinguished quests guests indeed my name is Yan Dalin probably best known for Skype. I am a technologist and I do believe in the potential of technology to make the world better. Coding and internet changed my life as well as my country. My country of Estonia has been pretty much built on the foundation of technology. so in India too digit digital technologies are playing a big role in growing the economy and making life better. connecting people and creating jobs. But as Stuart Russell now just highlighted, AI and by when I say AI, I mean the frontier models, the current LLM paradigm. AI is fundamentally different because that technology is not coded. It is trained. It is trained in a way that is difficult to understand and control. So right now AI is mostly a tool that helps us in the same ways. It makes us more productive and it makes us our lives easier. Right now AI seems to be a tool but will it stay that way?

a way that is difficult to understand and control. So right now AI is mostly a tool that helps us in the same ways. It makes us more productive and it makes us our lives easier. Right now AI seems to be a tool but will it stay that way? humanity is coming up to some pretty big decisions. These are the most important choices of our generation. What decisions? The big ones are what do we as humanity want from AI? What benefits are we expecting and creating? And secondly, even more importantly, what level of risk are we collectively willing to accept? So these are decisions that obviously affect the entire global community. Yet right now they are being made by a number of CEOs that could fit in an elevator on behalf of of 8 billion humans. It shouldn't be that way. And in fact, if you ask those CEOs in elevator, they point to each other and said like, "Oh, I can't my hands are tied because the other other guys are are racing." So in order for AI to remain at the tool that you want to be, a tool that unlocks the benefits For us, we need to stay in their driving seat. Yet, hundreds of billions or as Stuart points out now, trillions are going into developing AIs that will be agents and will have more autonomy. For a while, they'll become even more useful tools by being more independent, but jobs will definitely disappear. There are many digital tasks that become automatable if an agent can successfully work for several hours. And currently we are roughly around an hour mark. And that sure will be good for some people, but maybe not for a lot of people, especially in countries that rely on a lot of digital and remote work. And eventually the AI will be good enough at long and complex tasks that they can take over work in the sciences and in the AI research itself. At that point the biggest bottleneck that keeps AI progress at human understandable speeds humans doing the work disappears. Then the world starts running at machine speeds. What happens then? During award winner, Yosha Benjio has argued that if we build powerful agents, the risk that they will go rogue cannot be dismissed. But we have another choice. Imagine the possibilities if we get this right. We can build a future where AI amplifies human potential instead of replacing us. Where AI remains our most powerful tool and we remain in control. We can choose a safer path. one where AI remains humanity's most powerful tool developed with careful guard guard rails and oversight. Now, this isn't really about slowing progress. It's about smart progress where we don't try to develop human level intelligence or super intelligence without proper safety measures and where a few people don't rule roll the dice on all our lives. We can build AI systems that are

and oversight. Now, this isn't really about slowing progress. It's about smart progress where we don't try to develop human level intelligence or super intelligence without proper safety measures and where a few people don't rule roll the dice on all our lives. We can build AI systems that are with human values. AI that enhances human decision making instead of replacing it entirely. Systems we can understand, control, have visibility over wherever we are on the planet, and systems we can shut down if necessary. Again, the benefits could be extraordinary and safety achieved. AI could help us cure diseases while doctors remain in charge of treatment. It could solve climate change while engineers overseas solutions. It could accelerate scientific discovery while researchers guide the direction. Safety doesn't limit the AI's potential. It unlocks it responsibly. Now, as we gathered here, we know next year India hosts the AI summit. The world's governments and the CEOs of the world's leading companies will convene here. This is our chance to choose safety without sacrificing progress or inclusion. It's a chance to make a difference to call for these decisions to be made by humanity, not just by those CEOs in the elevator. India can lead in in the world in building AI we can trust. I hope India sees the opportunity because with right safe, rights, AI will transform our world for the better. just like the other technologies because as they rightly say, we are only one people on one planet and we stand for progress. Thank you. [Applause] Thank you so much sir. Now I would like to request principal scientific adviser to the government of India, Professor Ajay Kumar Sud to share his insights. Honorable Minister Shri Ashwini Vashnav GI and all the other dignitaries on the DAS off the dash experts in the audience from all sectors and all the well-wishers of artificial intelligence but also also the skeptics of AI. it's really a wonderful moment at this time when we are really launching the AI impact summit which will be happening very soon 2026 in India. Needless to say that this is a landmark moment where India can play a very significant role in this evolving technology. So if you see what our honorable prime minister quoted in Paris in 2025, I'll just repeat because it just says what AI stands for. He said quote AI is writing the code for humanity in this century. So this is one of the should I say pathbreaking technology disruptive technology which is not one technology in in itself but a culminate combination of many of them which is making a huge impact for human beings. Now this AI summit which will be happening here in February 2026 will be the first global AI summit in the in the global south. this AI summit which will be happening here in February 2026 will be the first global AI summit in the in the global south. So that has a very significant role as its mandate or what it will deliver because India traditionally has always positioned itself as a bridge between aligning our priorities whatever we have and the inclusive growth with the global conversations on technologies. So this AI summit happening in Delhi in February 2026 is extremely important from this point of view also. Now there are many aspects of AI which have been already mentioned by previous speakers and the AI mission how it is making all the difference. I remember few months back we were in the very hall when AI kosh was announced and the four big projects were funded for the LLMs. Now today again another few of them will be unveiled. All this is telling us that what we need to do is to move at speed and on scale. Now one aspect which is very important again which was brought out is the safety aspect the regulation aspect but without really curbing the innovation. Now keeping that in mind as you know that there was a committee constituted by mighty honorable minister under my leadership under my chairmanship and we had experts from all sectors in this to really come out with the framework which will look at all these aspects in a holistic manner. And today before lunch we had our final meeting. today which is resulting into culmination of what what India stands for and this document is really a result of the work of many many people who are in this hall who are on the stage and also the consultations which were done after this preliminary report was was put up on the website almost 2500 00 inputs were received out of which 600 were curated more and out of that what suggestions were made have been incorporated in a very positive manner in this report. So this report in my view will not only be serving India but it also will be a kind of a report which other countries will be interested in to see how you can marry innovation and regulation together. They need not be separate. So I will not go into what has gone into that report that will take me away from all this but very soon it will it will be launched by mighty. So I think at this stage I was just listen when I was listening to u the wisdom from the expert professor Russell. this definitely has many points to point ponder but one thing I thought I should mention that the AI mission at this stage is really not addressing or even looking at the aspects for AGI or artificial super intelligence. Our focus is really not on that at this stage. We are really seeing how we really take AI to the people, how

thought I should mention that the AI mission at this stage is really not addressing or even looking at the aspects for AGI or artificial super intelligence. Our focus is really not on that at this stage. We are really seeing how we really take AI to the people, how how we increase the efficiency of whatever we do. So our first cut is on that aspect but the dangers you pointed out are absolutely there and we need to address them. One thing I was just wondering the one aspect I'm sure in coming years experts like you will address because when we are looking at this AGI or whatever we call it a super intelligence and so on what we do not talk about is the energy need for all this because we only have finite energy and if this all these efforts take a huge portion of energy we need to really worry about the sustainability from that point. So in that sense another technology which is co-developing in the last 10 years is the quantum computing which is not anymore in the realm of just talking about it. there are quantum computing resources available on the web and it is increasing by the day and I don't think it will be too far when we will have 1 million quantum 1 million cubit quantum computer which is fault tolerant available if that happens I'm just saying if that happens and I think people like me think it will happen maybe it will be topology ological cubits maybe it will be the spin cubits maybe photonic cubits I don't know the answer but if that happens I think that time this artificial intelligence will have a real disruptive change the way it will develop and the way it will deliver so I'm hoping for that moment I'm sure it will happen sooner than later and in India we have efforts on the quantum computing front in a modest way. We have the AI mission and we have all the positive sense of how these technologies can be beneficial to the society. So with all this let me congratulate once again mighty AI mission and the leadership here on the dis who are really making this happen. almost on day-to-day basis we are moving forward.
